CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Treland unfortunately is not yet very distinguished as

a maker of motor cars, but there is a big business done
in repairs and hiring. Even some members of the Royal
Commission on Congestion in Ireland were whirled about

. the West during last winter on motor cars hired out
from an Irish firm. But the contract for motoring
during the spring and summer has been given to a
London firm, and, no doubt, Cockney chaffeurs who
know nothing of the country will be “dumped ’’ over
_here also. Tenders were asked on behalf of the Royal
" Commission on Congestion for the hire of three or four
cars for upwards of one hundred days spread over cer-
tain specified periods. ‘At £4 a day, which, we under-
stand, would not be an exorbitant price to charge when
everything, including drivers’ fees and subsistence
allowance, insurance of cars, ctc., are included, the total
, would come to £1,600 for 100 days for four

cars. The contract, therefore, is one well worthy the
consideration of the various Irish firms that trade in the
hiring out of motor cars, and there was a contract from
Ireland for less than £4 a day. As we have said, an
Irish firm supplied cars during the winter, and so the
Royal Commissioners had experience of what -an Irish
firm could do. But the present big contract has gone
to the firm of Brown Bros., Ltd., of London. We
would like to know how much lower, if anything lower,
was the tender of Messrs. Brown Bros. than the lowest
Ivish tender? Messrs. Brown Bros., Ltd., are- whole-
sale people, and the Irish firms whom they have com-
peted against and knocked out in this mere hiring con-
tract are, we suppose, customers of theirs. Several
Irish firms, we understand, competed for this hiring
contract, and the price at which the London firm got
it is rumoured to be only very little below that of am
Irish tender. We would like to hear the exact figure.
But the Royal Commission has had experience of an
Irish firm whose employees, being Irich, know the
country, yet they give the contract to a London firm.
Perhaps the Secretary of the Royal Commission on Con-
gestion in Ireland would throw light on this latesf;
“dump.”

Some years ago we had a few things to say about the
staffing of the Grand Canal Co. The position of engi-
neer to this company is now vacant, though we have
not seen the vacancy advertised. @ We wonder how
many candidates are in the running for it, and are any
of them would-be ‘‘dumps?’ We have reason te
believe that there are a few Irishmen looking for the
place, and we trust that the directors will be able to
suit, themselves without dumping an imported engineer
over here. It is just as well, however, to direct the
attention of the public to the vacancy before it is filled
up, so that the directors may know that an interest is
taken in the business. The previous engineer, who, we
hear, has been appointed to a much more important
position, and has therefore left the service of the Grand'
Canal Co., is an Irishman.

Recently Mr. Delany questioned Mr. Buxton, the
autocrat of the Post Office, concerning the stopping of
the annual increments of several officers in Dublin.
One of the postal officials who had his increment of
£5 4s. stopped had not, it appears, a single late attend-
ance in the course of a year—and two minutes after the
scheduled time constitutes, we understand, a late
attendance, but he had five minor irregularities alleged
against him—and any accidental slip in the performance
of a person’s duty constitutes, we understand, a minor
irregularity ; and even a ‘ hasty word,”” we believe,
comes within the category of *serious’’ offences. The
officer that Mr. Delany inquired about apparently had
his increment of £5 4s. stopped until such time as the
local martinets consider that he has purged his offence.
Mr. Buxton, of course, had no reason to think that the
Post, Office staff at Dublin was treated differently from
the staff at other offices—of course' not. Mr. Buxton
was careful not to specify the nature of the “serious
irregularities.’”

So long as British ministers like Mr. Sydney Buxb?,n F
are allowed to get off with answers of this sort, we don't
seco much hope of redress for those whose cases are
made the subjects of questions “on the floor of the
British House of Commons.”” Why are not these ques-
tions followed up by a series of supplemental questions
that might draw the British minister out of the groove
prepared for him by his permanent servants or martinets

who prepare the replies for him? The questioners in
these cases should worry the British minister as an Irish
terrier would worry = British rodent

We take the following from the Belfast ¢ Evening
Telegraph  of April 24th:—Twenty tons of the
material required for the construction of the Belfast
Cathedral new organ were conveyed to the city to-day.
_There will be a further consignment of six tons.” Our
Belfast contemporary places this news under the head-
ing “Local Brevities!’”” Twenty-six tons of imported
organ is a funny sort of a “ brevity,”” and we cortainly
fail to see where the “local”’ comes in. So far as
weight is concerned, the organ of the non-Catholic
“ Cathedral ”’ in Belfast is going to be a ponderous
affair. Needless to say, the “Saved’’ of Belfast are
importing the stuff from the “mother country ’—'appy
Hengland. When the twenty-six tons of English mate-
rial are in full blast we suppose the British exiles in
Belfast will hear the East a-calling!

How are consumers to support Irish cement if there
is not enough to go round? The North Dublin Guar-
dians ordered three tons of Irish cement, but the con-
tractor got a letter from the Irish cement makers in
Wexford—the ouly Irish cement manufacturers in lIve-
land, so far as we know—stating that they had no
cement to offer, ‘“all our make being sold ahead for
some time.”” There was a great deal of talk and dis
cussion some time ago about Irish cement, but yet
withal a Board of Guardians are unable to buy three
tons of it. Why do not some more Irishmen go into
the cement-making industry?

We learn from the ‘“New York World’’ of April
15th that ten men charged with hurling rotten eggs in
the Orpheum Theatre, Brooklyn, at certain performers
called Russell Brothers, were discharged. It appears
that ‘“one of the Russells—Jimmy—dressed as a ser-
vant girl, kicked around in a way that grew odious
even on the Bowery years ago, and how expressions
were used that had a reversible, double-action meaning
quite unlike anything that could be charged up against
a God-fearing Irishwoman.”” Mr. Justice Fleming said—
““ My impression of that production is very bad.  The
public should not encourage or patronise such plays.
No man of blood, particularly of Irish blood, could sit
and listen to anyone who would thus disgrace the women
of his race. I am satisfied from the testimony that
this act was indecent, shocking and vulgar to the last
degree.”” The cases against the ten men who pelted
these playboys of the Western World were dismissed.

A third edition of the Vocabulary to Ta0x Taba,
compiled by ‘“a Limerick teacher,”” and published by
O’Mahony and Co., Limerick, has just been published.
We hear that the sale of this very useful Vocabulary is
increasing daily.

They had a bazaar in Newry in connection with the
Newry Rowing Club. A few ‘‘sweepstakes’’ were
organised—we wonder what were the police doing?
One of the sweeps was for a race called the Derby,
and the ‘“grand prize”’ of that sweep was £250.
There was ‘“ A Palace of Nations’’ from which Ireland
was left out in the cold. The bazaar was evidently
very much “ all-creeds-all-classes,”” so perhaps it would
‘have been bigoted and political to drag in Ireland!
Ttaly, Norway, and India were represented, but Ireland
was_ignored in the Newry Rowing Club Bazaar; very
_probably Ireland is ignored all the year round in Newry.
The bazaar was opened by Mr. Henry Loughran, J.P.,
Chairman of the Newry Urban Council. ' We read

May 4, 1907.

that ‘“the decorators of the hall placed as back-
ground a lovely scene depicting the Prince of
Wales’ visit to the:Delhi, Durbar, the picturesque pro-
cession being admirably pourtrayed.””  TIreland and
Trish are evidently at a discount in Newry. We have
no doubt the bazaar, with its “ palmist >’ and roulettes,
and so forth, attracted the ““ cream ’’ of Newry society !
On Monday, April 22nd, Father Phelan, the well-
known Jesuit preacher, delivered an address on the fur-
thorance of Temperance and the supporting of Irish
industries; but those who consider themselves the
“cream '’ of Newry society were very sparsely repre-
sented. But then there is an explanation: some stroll-
ing company of English players produced ‘Charley's
Aunt”’ that night at the Newry Town Hall.

can get 1,000 programmes in England for 9s. Cur
members aré advocates of the Irish Treland movement;
but even the most advanced member of that cause will
not lie under such exorbitant prices. Under these cir-
cumstances we cannot see our way to throw away money
for sentiment. Perhaps the Clonmel Typographical
Society would ask their brothers in England for an
explanation of their low quotations, and not blame the
Dramatic Company for accepting the lowest tender. As
long as the people of Ireland are asked to make such
sacrifices to support the industries of our country, they
cannot develop side by side. That copies of resolu-
tion be sent to the local Press, ‘ The Nationalist,” ¢ The
Champion,” Tue Leaper, and the Clonmel Develop-

jealousy than nationality at the back of it, for two of
a kind can never agree.”’—John Nolan, IHon. Secre-
tary.

crippled father and an aged mother. This case was so
deserving, and the poor people so dreadfully in want, -

spirit. At a meeting of a provincial Urban Council
the Chairman, Mr. M. J. Hughes, J.P., complained of
scavengers lifting their hats to members of the Urban
Council. This was most degrading, he said, and should
be put a stop to. Mr. Barry declared that he would
fine any man a month’s pay who lifted his hat to a
Councillor, and Mr. Smith declared that there was an
order on the books that any man found coramitting
this offence should be dismissed. Mr. Lennon takes a
pessimistic view of the situation.  We will never get
independence into them,”” he said. Mr. Hughes, J.P.,
said—*“ I would take off my hat to no one but a clergy-
man.””  Mr. O’Connell gallantly inquired—‘ What
ahout-the ladies?”’ ‘“That is a different thing,”” said

What a cry there would be if a few Unionisti were
set upon in any part of ‘“the West and South’’ that
the ““loyal”’ orators love to orate about. It appears
that a member of the Irish Parliamentary Party, M'r.
David Sheehy, delivered an address at a meeting in
Portadown, and the advisability of forming a local
branch of the United Irish League was discussed. Let
us see what one of the local pillars of ‘“liberty an,q
the Union think of it.  The ‘“ Portadown Express
SilyS R

Wh we have no desire to stir up anything in the matter of
party strife, we think a word of warning may not be out of place.
The United Irish League is an institution disloyal to the core; its
ian methods in the South and West have been such as to
make its name stink in tho nostrils of the more self-respecting
Roman Catholics, and if these methods are to be imported to
Portadown then we can tell Mr. Shechy and his compatriots in
vulgar parlance that they are “coming to the wrong shop.”
Portadown is happily very free from anything in the nature of
sectarian strife; let us hope it will remain so.

There was a ‘‘ great display of amateur talent’’ at

the Boyle military barracks one night last week. The
hall, of course, ‘“was thronged to suffocation’’ with a
‘‘ very representative audience.’”” The ‘‘ comics’’ of
the occasions were one Paddy O’Keefie and Sergeant
Byrne. Private Patrick Connell sang ‘‘ In the Shade
of the Old Apple Tree,”’ and Lieutenants Tibbs sang
“ Zuyder Zee.”” Paddy O’Keeffe, who was attired in a
clerical costume, ‘‘ kept the house in roars of laughter;’’
he sang “ More Work for the Undertaker.”” Sergeant
Byrne, atlired in kilts, sang ‘“ A Tanner a Pint for
Fourpenny Ale.”” We are told that ‘“ his appearance
is nothing new on the st 1 Boyle, and on every occa-
sion he has the good fortune of fastening another link
to an already high reputation as a comedian.” We are
not informed, however, where-he fastens the link ; does
he put it through his nose, we wonder? Private Mul-
lany apparently acted the part of the Stage Irishwoman ;
well for him perhaps that it was in Boyle, and not in
Brooklyn, he was reciting. It appears a ‘‘ colleen ”’
was charged in the police court with drinking teo much
of the ‘“crathur.” The magistrate, according to the
piece, asked the ‘colleen’’ if she ‘‘ drank beer with
impunity #’  ““ No,” was the reply; ‘“only with Mrs.
Zelly.  ““She must be a liberal woman,”’ said the
magistrate. ‘“ No, she’s a Roman Catholic,”” was the
response.  We suppose this is just the sort of thing
that would go down with some of those who like bar-
rack concerts in Boyle.

Messrs. Carroll and Co., of Dundalk, the well-known
Trish tobacco manufacturers, are doing splendidly in
spite of the Combine. Our contemporary, the ‘‘ Dun-
dalk Democrat,”” in the course of a note remarks:—
“Tt is a pleasure to us {o know that Messrs. Carroll

C , S0 far from having been extinguished by the
perial Tobacco Combine, as some people were pro-
Phesying at the time for them, and for all other small
manufacturers, have vastly increased their trade during
th_e past few years, so much so that their factory, built
within the past twenty years, has become altogether too
small for the present requirements of their trade, and
we understand that Messrs. Carroll have acquired a
tract of land in the demesne, adjoining their premises,
for the erection of new factory buildings. In connec-
tion with this it is also stated that Mr. V. S. Carroll
has taken over the Demesne House, formerly the Dun-
da.llf residence of the Earls of Roden, and lately in occu-
pation of the agent of the property and of Mr, J. M.
Cox, at present.”” The Irish tobacco manufacturers
have made a sturdy fight, and deserve the hearty sup-
port of the Irish smoking public. We only wish that
all other classes of Irish manufacturers were as business-
like and as enterprising.

Irish Treland owes a great deal to the members of the
Redemptorist Order in Ireland.  Their influence as
preachers and missionaries give great importance to
their words when they plead, as they often do, for the
support of the language and industry of Ireland. One
of the Order, a powerful preacher in both Irish and
English, and a prominent advocate of Irish ideas, speak-
ing at the close of a retreat to about 2,000 Belfast men
on Sunday week, urged the claims of Irish goods and
Irish literature, and appealed to them to at least see
that their children were taught Irish in the schools if
they could not see their way to learn Irish themselves.
Speaking of the Press the preacher recommended the
LEADER, whose work he kindly eulogised.

The Meath Feis—[eip na Mive—will be held on
the 29th and 30th of June next. There will be literary,
history, singing, dancing, band, inter-branch and other
competitions. ~ An industrial section will also be a
feature of the ferp.

Mr. Edward Martyn is probably getting rather bored
playing the unconvincing pant of leader presumptive of
the Irish race at home and abroad. Mr. Martyn, had
he been wise, which men born to too much money some-
times are not, would never have intruded into the field
of politics. ‘He would have kept to play-writing; he
might have been a first-class dramatist; he can never
be more than a ridiculous politician.

Mr. Martyn has written a letter evidently inspired
by the reading of the speeches of our distinguished
weekly Irish contributor, Father Dinneen. Inci-
dentally he remarks that “no man knows the state of
the language movement in Ireland better than Father
Dinneen.”” Mr. Martyn says:—"“ It will not merely do
for the governing body of the Gaelic League to keep
chattering around a table, and carrying on the old busi-

_ mness of patching up squabbles of branches at a some-
what prolix expenditure of argument.”’ This, by the
way, 18 rather a “ back-hander’’ at his colleagues on

. the Executive Committee. How far it may be justified,
if it be justified in any degree, we cannot say, as the
“governing body ’’ confer in private; and whether
their  chattering,”” to use Mr. Martyn’s rather irre-
verent word, is foolish or wise, we cannot preciselv
state. Mr. Martyn suggested that a troupe of Irish
strolling players should be organised. It is a very good
suggestion, though we do not think it is a new one. In

- fact we think it is rather an old and hardy suggestion.
In any case it is an obvious suggestion ; but Mr. Martyn
announces that he is prepared to subscribe liberally to
the project, and that announcement coming from a gen-

‘tleman who is credited with having given £10,000
towards the upkeep of a Palestrina choir, is most
decidedly important.

Mr. Edward Martyn also suggests a translation into
h of some master-piece. He says that “It is
nerally believed that what started German literature
he translation of the Bible.”” Some of his politi-
tenants ought to be able to inform him that

| May 4, 1907,

there is a translation Cor alleged. bra.nslwbipn, “of ‘the
Bi(l}nle in Irish extant—the Libraries ‘Committee of the
Dublin Corporation begged some copies for nothing,
and duly received them!

Mr. Edward Martyn, who may be more than an
0’Growneyite for all we know to the contrary, is rather
hard on the writers of Irish. He_s?lys:—‘: Of course,
somo will say that wo have many original writers now in
Irish. We have, no doubt; but somehow the people
with interesting ideas do not know Irish at all or well
enough, and those who know Irish do not appear to
have very much to say.”” This is rather hard, a’nd we
would venture to say grossly unjust, on Canon O’Leary
Tather Dinneen, and several others, including some of
Mr. Martyn’s colleagues on the Coirte BJnot.. We
have reason to believe that Mr. Martyn reads the
LEADER every weoek ; surely he cannot be serious in sug-
gesting that, the oxcellent and topical article in Irish
by Father Dinneen are the work of a man who does
“not appear to have very much to say.”” Mr. Martyn
remarks:—“In any case we have not as yet any work
in modern Irish that could, without inviting the world’s
ridicule, be called a world’s master-work; and without
a master-work in its literature, no language can sur-
vive.””  We should have thought that there were at
least twenty authors of master-pieces amongst Mr. Mar-
tyn’s colleagues on the Executive Committes!  Mr.
Martyn had better take care, or he may find “ back-
handers”’ at his “chattering ’’ colleagues even more
risky than the dubious position of leader presumptive
of the Irish race at home and abroad!

We are informed that the stationmaster at Emly, ou
the G.S. and W. Railway, is likewise an Emigration
Agent. Do the directors of the G.S. and W. Railway
allow their stationmasters to act as Emigration Agents?

Our contemporary, the ‘“ Midland Tribune,”” pub-
lishes a very interesting eight-page supplement, ‘‘Oan
na nFaeveal,” every week. The last issue was the
28th, so that the supplement is over six months run-
ning. Indeed, the supplement by itself is excellent
value for a penny, though it is given away free with the
‘“ Midland Tribune.” 1In the last issue it refers to the
harm done to the Gaelic League by the League getting
mixed up in the minds of some people with Mr. Mar-
tyn’s political party. It finishes up its reference with
the remark—‘‘Our esteemed contemporary, the LEADER,
Dublin, was the pioneer of the real Sinn Fein move-
ment.” Amongst other articles in “ Oan na nFaeveal”
is one by Micéal O Meacaip on the Industrial
Revival. In the course of the article we read:—
“ Already it is satisfactory to mnotice the improvement
and acceleration in the turn out of Irish goods since the
revival movement begun, and the manufacturers given
the necessary encouragement by the increased demand,
brought about by the focussing of public opinion on this
plain patriotic wisdom, largely through the influence
of the Gaelic League and the constant expounding of
the LeapEr, the first and still the only Industrial Re-
vival newspaper.’’

The Clara Monastery Dramatic Class recently pro-
duced Mr. Mangan’s play, ‘‘ Robert Emmet.”” = Clara
is a village in the King’s County, and the boys of the
Franciscan Brothers’ Monastery are naturally nearly
all drawn from the labouring classes It was certainly
a remarkable achievement on the part of the Franciscan
Brothers of Clara to have this play staged by their

THE LEADER.

THE FAIR HILLS OF EIRE.

Last week we published & beautiful poem in French
written by a French priest in exile from his.native
land. A reader of the LEADER, Mr. Felix MacSwiney,
Birmingham, writes us :-—‘ Herewith I send an attempt
at a metrical translation into English of ‘The Fair
Hills of Eire’ from last week’s LEADER.”

The hills of Ireland shelter lakes as blue,
Reflecting azure skies like crystal pure;

As if God willed with skill and taste so true,
’Mong finest pearls some sapphires to secure.

Green Ireland’s fields are decked with rarest flowers,
Her shores indented by the fretting wave,

Her smiling valleys wet with softest showers,
Hills undulate 'neath heavens pure and grave.

She has her boundless plains o’er which the breeze,
Warmed by the purple rays of evening sun,

Blows softly, bearing sweetest harmonies,
Bespreading perfumes in its passage won.

Around her coasts stands many a tall cliff’s crest,
Which weary feet of birds of passage seek;

Where summer swallow builds its giddy nest,
And winter snows lie late and cold and bleak.

Laving her shores, extends the western sea:
The ocean in its sleep immense and calm;
Where purple rays of eve o’er blue waves free
The coming night with mystic thoughts embalm.

She has her memories—her dismantled fanes;
Traces of glory, always noble, grand;

Where holy men long laboured, whose remains
Lie scattered 'mongst the ruins of this land.

She has her memories—O! those bards of old!
Whose golden harps did warrior hymns vibrate;
And haughty Celtic heroes brave and bold—
How far has Eire fallen from high estate!

But still her soul’s unstained, her spirit’s free:
That soul responsive to the slightest nod

Of Faith—like harp-strings touched by hands we see—
Still sweetly vibrates to the hand of God.

This noble Ireland I salute in you

Through whom she makes an exile’s pang less keem:
A manly type of her best virtues true—

A poor return this feeble song I ween.

Ye, generous hearts of Eire, fain I'd greet,
Who stranger’s sorrow softened for a day,
To think of you with grateful heart is meet
And for your welfare ever hope and pray.

movements—political or social.  For, as a rule, the
genuine Gaelic League worker has had very little time

gious sects. But when the Gaelic Leaguer persisted in
calling for a contribution year after year many people
felt that he was becoming a nuisance, and dealt with
him accordingly. It was at this stage the Gaelic
Leaguer found himself face to face with difficulties

siasts can, by virtue of a fremendous amount of energy

ing, because their whole power is exhausted in one

“THE SPOILED PRIEST.”

OULD-BE sages often shake their heads and say

“ He is a spoiled priest,”” in a tone vhat would

imply that such a judgment is cnough “o damn any

man. When you ¢ hem to tesk about this general

condemnation, ax 1% oub living contradictions—and

happily there are very many in ovr midst—of their con-

viclions, they are stuined more at your daring than at

your arguments. Accordingly, I will venture to tres-

pass on the reader’s patienze to discuss, as briefly as T
can, the matter.

A spoiled priest—the very term itself is a misdescrip-
tion, and conveys a false impression. It does not sig-
nify an unfrocked priest, as a stranger to the term might
at first suppose. It signifies a man who, it may be, for
conscience’ sake, abandons a calling, .at once remunera-
tive and with good prospects, for a dreary future in
which both prospect and remuneration are very dubious.
We do not speak of Forbes-Robertson, or William
Black as spoiled artists because the one abandoned the
casel for the buskin, the other the pencil for the pen.
We discount neither Robertson’s “ Hamlet” nor
Black’s “ Madeap Violet ’’ because the actor in the one
caso and the author in the other dallied a while at
another calling. Mark Twain is not a spoiled composi-
tor because Samuel Clemens left down his “ stick > and.
lives by his humorous writings. Henry Irving was not
a spoiled clerk, nor is Barton M‘Guckin one. Yet one
became an actor; the other a singer. John Redmond is
not a spoiled Treasury Clerk, nor was Harcourt a spoiled
lawyer, because fame came to both as members of Par-
liament. I look around at the men most prominent in
the public eye for the moment—everywhere the story is
the same; I have but taken a few examples at random.
Many have not found their proper calling at the first
attempt. We draw many a blank before we reach a
lucky number. Nor is the analogy here suggested per-
fect. The men I have named have actually followed
one calling for a time before embarking on the other
which proved the more appropriate. Your young semi-
narist who leaves the seminary has merely spent a few
years at most in training for a profession. He has not
reached the profession itself. He has been studying
for it much as the law student studies for the Bar, or
the medical for his profession. No stigma attaches to
any but an ecclesiastical student who changes his mind.
Why is this so?

But it is so, and it is passing strange that the man
who rightly or wrongly regards himself as having a
vocation for the immediate service of God should, in
the public mind, be chained to that immediate service
instantly and irretrievably, Take a case—A. boy iz more
than ordinarily studious and well-behaved, and the
superiors of the school regard him as a likely levite.
They fill his mind with the idea, and he is naturally
charmed with it. He is really too young as yet to form
a decision of such importance for himself, and must be
largely determined in his choice by others.  To this
may be added the confusing element of emotional piety
which: often appears about this period.  Emotional
piety is useful in the spiritual life in order to aid the
weakly in the carly stages of their: progress.  But it
is not calculated to aid towards a clear judgment in the
serious matter of a religious vocation. Hence a passing
fancy, or mayhap a well-intentioned desire, is apt, under
such circumstances, to be interpreted as a vocation.

When he enters a seminary the boy is about fifteen
years of age. Yet his judgment, formed at that imma-
ture age is by the world regarded as binding, though
not so regarded by his religious superiors.  For the
moment he enters a seminary he is regarded by the
world as having left the world; if he ever returns, be
ﬂhe‘cz\usn what it may, he is branded (I use the word
advisedly) as a spoiled priest. THence the misfits that
we occasionally encounter in the ecclesiastical world, and
. who give so much trouble to their superiors and so much
scandal to weaklings.

Now, on the other side, the case is very different.
T'rom the day he enters the seminavy it is the duty of
his superiors to question in every way his fitness and
test ‘his alleged vocation. While this is going forward,
his character is being formed, and his ultimate decision
and the ultimate decision of lhis superiors is largely in-
fluenced by the character thus forming. I may here
remark in parenthesis that I have often thought what a
much better world it would be if all our Catholic young
men could spend a few years in a seminary living accord-
ing to its strict rule and discipline. Each Buddhist male
must, I have heard, spend three years in a monastery
living according to a very rigorous rule. I have always
admired that custom.

As a result of our levite’s experience he either proves
himself unfitted for the state he aspires to, or makes it
evident that lhie has no real vocation, or loses or goes in
opposition to his vocation.

There are men who are absolutely unable to pursue
the necessary studies tihrough lack of brains.  There
are men of fair promise and bad performance. There
are men whose: characters develop vicious tendencies;
there are those who do not correspond to their oppor-
tunities. All these should eventually leave the semi-
nary. There are men who, on probation, show clearly
that they have no real vocation. Now if such a man is
weak-minded, and shrinks from the alternative of becom-
i ing a spoiled priest on account of the odium attaching to
i the name, what is the result? He shilly-shallies
H through his cowrse, dallies at the fatal moment, and,

3 while unsuited, becomes a minister of thio gospel. And
if there is a life more full of dread possibilities, more
absolutely repugnant to mere natural feelings, more
awful in its present and future responsibilities than
that of an anointed minister of God who has no true
. vocation, I cannot, by even the greatest stretch of
imagination, conceive it. Of course, all the while T
am speaking of a Catholic minister of the gospel.
Many of these men judged by the ordinary tests of
the world are really excellent men. The English Chan-
cellor—Blessed John More—decided that for himself it
was better to be a good layman than possibly an indif-
ferent priest—he proved it too. The father of the
great French Jesuit, Bourdaloue, gave to the Church
one of her greatest preachers, though the father him-

self was a spoiled priest. I know an instance of a

man who when a young lad entered a seminary full of

mighty fervour for his calling. His enthusiasm was
damped, however, when he realised that among his
class-fellows the same ecstacy did not obtain, and as he

did not then realise that priests arc men and not angels

he returned to the world.” That man married, and before

the died had the great pleasure of seeing his whole family
~ consecrated to the service of God’s altar. I have said
sufficient on this point, I am sure, and will not insult
the intelligence of the reader by doing all his thinking
for him. I always consider it quite sufficient to sug-
gest the points of a subject, and leave it to the readers
to do the rest. A man who is not able to think for him-
self will not read articles in Tnr LeApEr; and my
remarks are directed not to the emotions of the un-
thinking, but to the intelligence of the thinking man.

This volume is a plain, unimpassioned account of the-
Puritan persecutions in Ireland. The sources of infor-
mation to which the author has gone are of unimpeach-
able authenticity. ~ He has drawn very largely from:
documents written by contemporaries of those who
were the victims of the Puritan tyranny, and in not a
few cases his Eminence reproduces thie written testimony
of many who themselves suffered under the Cromwellian
reign of terror. In this book, therefore, we have a
simple record of the truth. We do not see put forward,
as in some histories dealing with the period, any hpyo-
theses, whether well-grounded or baseless, any surmises,
suggestions, or conjectures, whether plausible or absurd.
It is a story constructed on evidence which cannot be
disputed, a history complete in every detail, and forming
altogether a picture of unrelieved diabolical cruelty that
one cannot contemplate without an instinctive shudder.

When Cromwell and his battalions landed in Ireland
in 1649, they were flushed with the signal success with
which they had just terminated the war with the ill-
fated Charles. They came to Ireland with blood upon
their hands, and it might have been confidently expected
that should the hated Irish: Papists fall into their power
the Puritans would certainly treat them with mno dis-

Such: an expectation would, indeed, have come to a
terrible realisation. The beginning of the Cromwellian
campaign in Ireland was the inception of a crusade for
the extirpation of the Irish race. To achieve this end
no means that an ingenious brutality could suggest was
left untried. ~Massacre and burnings, plunder and tor-
ture, banishment and every conceivable form of persecu-
tion were all carried out with a relentless ferocity that
gives to the atrocities of Nero, Diocletian, or Caligula a-
character of comparative mildness. Nor was there
shown any discrimination asto age or sex or the infirm—
young and old, men and women, the strong and the
feeble, all alike became the objects of Puritan fury.
And to no class in the community was this savage hos-

well known to Cromwell and his followers that the-:
powerful influence of the priests had always been put
forward in support of the people’s cause; and it was
further abundantly manifest that the exhortations and’
oxample of the clergy were a potent stimulus to their
flocks in tenaciously adhering to a religion which it was
the declared object of thePuritans to destroy. The priests

is determined to do somecthing respectable for
Inish music in the near future. On Easter Tuesday
last, chiefly, I understand, through the efforts of
Leaguers, if not of the Leaguo officially, wo were given
a sacred concert in which such artistes as Signorina
Constantini, Miss Lizzie Gorman, Signor Jean Cokkius,
and Mr. George Hodgson took part, and at which “ St.
Patrick’s Breastplate’’ was performed. Then last week
the recently organised Gaelic League choir, with Mr.
Carl Hardebeck as conductor, made its bow to the con-
cert-going public with a programme which the critics
have praised as a model of what an Trish concert pro-
gramme should be. Finally, on Saturday evening, Mr.
Reynolds of the local press, assisted by Mr. Hardebeck,
discussed in ““.4n Cpsod Ruad” the whole question
of the Gaelic League’s duty and responsibility in the
matter of the revival of Irish music and song. As the
lecture deserved a bigger audience than that which lis-
tened to it, and was fitted for perhaps a more Irish
audience than it will find in the Belfast papers, I am
tempted to try to give some idea of its more salient
points in the LEADER.

Starting with the proposition that the Gaelic League
was the strongest force making for nationality in Ire-
land, Mr. Reynolds suggested that its responsibility in
the matter of the revival of so large a part of nation-
ality as national music was great. He then proceeded
to say what he thought of Gaelic League concerts as
they are—of the inantistic get up of the programmes,
of the absence from them of classical foreign items, of
the mediocrity of the artistes, etc.—on all of which
he was courageously and, to those of the audience who
were not artistes themselves, charmingly frank. He,
however, did not blame the Gaelic League for these
defects, he merely contended that the League should,
almost at any cost, strive to raise the artistic standard
in things musical in Ireland. And to that end he
would have it encourage its members to educate them-
selves artistically; go to hear musical masterpieces, no
matter by whom given; make use of expert help, no
matter whose or whence the help, in its own functions;
finally, encourage by every means in their power any
young composer or artist who should chance to arise in
the country, and give him bread and butter to work
on, and something to work for, in his native land.

This cleared the ground for the disoussion of the
question “ What is Irish music?’ And here I fear T
may -not succeed in accurately reporting Mr. Reynold:.
In London, he said, there were numbers of clever versi-
fiers and composers who, by the judicious use of cer-
tain Irish words, and phrases, and of turns of melody
to be found in Irish tunes, could turn out work as like
Irish: work as two peas. It would be wonderfully like

would be disgusted with leaving my hero in the grip of
a red Indian, or perhaps falling down a cliff, or being

hero was ever worsted; never! Such a thing would

if a paper were started on either of the above lines? I
think there could. One way or another there is still a
good deal of talent in Ireland; and, aftter all, little
talent is required in the production of a boys' paper;
rather should the writer of boys’ stories be possessed of
a vivid imagination, able to depict in suitable Janguage
the many daring escapades through which his hero must
pass, and to laugh with his reader as the occasion
demands. Granted the supply, the next thing to con-
cider is wihich of the suggested models should be adopted.
Neither, I should say. At present, unforbupabcly,_xt

Another point that should be emphasised is the
absolute necessity for plenty of sterling school stories.
Of the many things that a boy will read with interest,
none claims more attention than the school story. Boys. -
never seem to tire of them, and though, to a certain
extent, there must always be a eimilarity—your hero,
your bully, and your weakling being almost inevitable:
and practically identical in all stories, yet somehow if it
is up to the average tihe boy will surely vote it “ripping.”” .
Proof of the popularity of the school story is demon-
strated by the fact that quite recently a London boys’
paper commenced issuing in book form, at threepence
each, sevoral of the stories which had appeared as serials
in its columns for some years past, and that while all
are having a large sale those dealing with school life
casily head the list. Little more remains to be said:
the format of the paper is not a very material point,
though the price, of course, should be the modest penny ;
tihe publication weekly, preferably about every Thurs-
day. The subject is one for serious thought; the want
is keenly felt, and as a lucrative investment the chances
are rosy. May the day be not far distant when our
Irish boys will find their heroes personified, not by a
glorified British lad, but by a boy who, like themselves,
can hoast of the proud Milesian blood in his veins!
